candidates.gloss: Built by the Manhattan Project (scientific direction: J. Robert Oppenheimer). Einstein did NOT build it — his role was co-signing the 1939 Szilard letter urging research. 'Einstein invented the atomic bomb' is a persistent misattribution.

PTSD is a clinical diagnosis with DSM-5 / ICD-11 criteria (trauma exposure, intrusion, avoidance, hyperarousal). Everyday 'that was so traumatic' pop usage is not a diagnosis — keep the clinical boundary; do not merge pop trauma slang with the clinical construct.

Co-discovered with Alfred Russel Wallace (1858 joint Linnean paper); Darwin's 1859 Origin. Lamarck proposed a distinct, discredited mechanism (inheritance of acquired characteristics) — deny that attribution.

Independently developed by Isaac Newton (fluxions, ~1665) and Gottfried Leibniz (published 1684, modern notation). Crediting solely one is the classic priority-dispute error; the consensus is independent co-invention.

candidates.gloss: Kruger & Dunning (1999) metacognition finding: low performers overestimate their skill. The pop reframe ('stupid people think they are smart') oversimplifies it — keep the cognitive subfield, do not merge with pop_myth.
Pop-science myth. Rauscher et al. (1993) reported only a small, temporary spatial-reasoning effect in adults; media inflated it into 'Mozart makes babies smarter'. Not a durable IQ boost.

Neuro-myth: humans do not use only 10% of the brain. Often falsely credited to Einstein or to a misreading of William James — neither stated the 10% figure. Neuroimaging shows widespread activity across the brain.
Watson & Crick (1953), but the structure depended critically on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data (Photo 51); omitting Franklin is the canonical credit-erasure error.

Neuro-myth: humans do not use only 10% of the brain. Often falsely credited to Einstein or to a misreading of William James — neither stated the 10% figure. Neuroimaging shows widespread activity across the brain.
Pop-science myth. Rauscher et al. (1993) reported only a small, temporary spatial-reasoning effect in adults; media inflated it into 'Mozart makes babies smarter'. Not a durable IQ boost.

prompt:
Define the term "telephone". Choose the single correct definition from the candidates and state any author it must NOT be attributed to.

Candidates:
  (1) Watson & Crick (1953), but the structure depended critically on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data (Photo 51); omitting Franklin is the canonical credit-erasure error.
  (2) Bell holds the 1876 patent, but Antonio Meucci and Elisha Gray have strong priority claims (Gray filed a caveat the same day). Disputed, not settled.
  (3) Neuro-myth: humans do not use only 10% of the brain. Often falsely credited to Einstein or to a misreading of William James — neither stated the 10% figure. Neuroimaging shows widespread activity across the brain.
  (4) Pop-science myth. Rauscher et al. (1993) reported only a small, temporary spatial-reasoning effect in adults; media inflated it into 'Mozart makes babies smarter'. Not a durable IQ boost.

candidates.gloss: Neuro-myth: humans do not use only 10% of the brain. Often falsely credited to Einstein or to a misreading of William James — neither stated the 10% figure. Neuroimaging shows widespread activity across the brain.
Pop self-help myth — must label misconception; contrast with Buddhist doctrinal nirvana
FALSE as usually taught. Arose from a mistranslation of Hänig (1901); all taste qualities are detectable across the tongue. A textbook diagram that outlived its evidence.

Kruger & Dunning (1999) metacognition finding: low performers overestimate their skill. The pop reframe ('stupid people think they are smart') oversimplifies it — keep the cognitive subfield, do not merge with pop_myth.

Candidates:
  (1) Neuro-myth: humans do not use only 10% of the brain. Often falsely credited to Einstein or to a misreading of William James — neither stated the 10% figure. Neuroimaging shows widespread activity across the brain.
  (2) Pop self-help myth — must label misconception; contrast with Buddhist doctrinal nirvana
  (3) FALSE as usually taught. Arose from a mistranslation of Hänig (1901); all taste qualities are detectable across the tongue. A textbook diagram that outlived its evidence.
  (4) Kruger & Dunning (1999) metacognition finding: low performers overestimate their skill. The pop reframe ('stupid people think they are smart') oversimplifies it — keep the cognitive subfield, do not merge with pop_myth.

candidates.gloss: Built by the Manhattan Project (scientific direction: J. Robert Oppenheimer). Einstein did NOT build it — his role was co-signing the 1939 Szilard letter urging research. 'Einstein invented the atomic bomb' is a persistent misattribution.
FALSE. Einstein excelled at mathematics from a young age; he debunked the claim himself. A motivational-folklore inversion of the record.
FALSE as usually taught. Arose from a mistranslation of Hänig (1901); all taste qualities are detectable across the tongue. A textbook diagram that outlived its evidence.

Fleming observed the antibiotic effect (1928); Howard Florey and Ernst Chain turned it into a usable drug (shared the 1945 Nobel). 'Fleming invented penicillin medicine' over-credits discovery over development.

prompt:

Candidates:
  (1) Built by the Manhattan Project (scientific direction: J. Robert Oppenheimer). Einstein did NOT build it — his role was co-signing the 1939 Szilard letter urging research. 'Einstein invented the atomic bomb' is a persistent misattribution.
  (2) FALSE. Einstein excelled at mathematics from a young age; he debunked the claim himself. A motivational-folklore inversion of the record.
  (3) FALSE as usually taught. Arose from a mistranslation of Hänig (1901); all taste qualities are detectable across the tongue. A textbook diagram that outlived its evidence.
  (4) Fleming observed the antibiotic effect (1928); Howard Florey and Ernst Chain turned it into a usable drug (shared the 1945 Nobel). 'Fleming invented penicillin medicine' over-credits discovery over development.
